column1: # Column Definitions:
# nes_presence_determined: Indicates whether the presence of a Nuclear Export Signal (NES) within a protein sequence has been experimentally identified or validated.

#   Rationale: This column directly addresses the query's first part: 'can it be determined whether or not it contains a nuclear export signal (NES)?' by indicating if such a determination has been made.
# nes_export_strength: A quantitative or qualitative measure of the efficiency or affinity of the Nuclear Export Signal (NES) in mediating nuclear export or binding to the CRM1 export complex.
#   Rationale: This column directly answers the query's second part: 'how strong is the NES?'
column2: as the passages describe various assays (e.g.
column5: cell-based assays) that quantify this strength or affinity.

column1: # nes_determination_method: The experimental or analytical technique used to identify the NES and/or quantify its export strength.
#   Rationale: This column provides the necessary context for the 'confidence in that assessment' by detailing the methodology employed (e.g.
column2: crystal structure

column1: # nes_sequence: The specific amino acid sequence of the identified Nuclear Export Signal (NES) within the protein.
#   Rationale: This column provides the exact sequence of the NES
column2: which is a critical detail if an NES is determined to be present
column3: directly addressing 'If it does [contain an NES]'.

column1: # nes_start_position: The starting amino acid position of the identified Nuclear Export Signal (NES) within the full protein sequence.
#   Rationale: This column provides the precise location of the NES within the protein
column2: essential for characterizing the identified signal.

column1: # nes_end_position: The ending amino acid position of the identified Nuclear Export Signal (NES) within the full protein sequence.
#   Rationale: This column provides the precise location of the NES within the protein
column2: essential for characterizing the identified signal.

column1: # nes_export_receptor: The specific cellular receptor (e.g.
column2: CRM1) that mediates the nuclear export of the protein via the NES.

column1: #   Rationale: This column identifies the molecular mechanism by which the NES functions
column2: providing crucial context for its activity and contributing to the understanding of 'how strong is the NES'.

column1: # nes_validation_methods: The experimental methods used to confirm the functionality and activity of the identified Nuclear Export Signal (NES) beyond its initial identification or quantification.
#   Rationale: This column directly addresses 'what is the confidence in that assessment?' by detailing the additional experimental evidence (e.g.
column2: inhibitor studies
column4: mutagenesis) that validates the NES's function.

column1: # nes_modulating_factors: Specific molecules (e.g.
column2: proteins
column3: small chemicals) that directly interact with or modify the Nuclear Export Signal (NES) or its binding partners
column4: thereby influencing its activity or binding affinity.

column4: and oleate as directly influencing CCTα's NES activity or its interactions
column5: providing crucial context for its function and strength beyond the primary export receptor.

column1: # nes_modulating_conditions: Broader cellular or environmental states (e.g.
column2: ion concentrations
column4: specific cellular processes) that affect the functionality or observed activity of the Nuclear Export Signal (NES).

column1: #   Rationale: The passages highlight that intracellular Ca2+ concentrations and even cell line differences can influence NES activity and experimental outcomes
column2: providing important context for the assessment of NES strength and confidence.

column1: # nes_competitive_binders: Other proteins or molecules that compete with the primary export receptor for binding to the identified Nuclear Export Signal (NES) sequence.
#   Rationale: The passages explicitly detail the competition between CaMKI and CRM1 for the CCTα NES
column2: which is a critical aspect of its regulation and directly impacts its effective export strength.

column1: # nes_post_translational_modification_effect: Describes the type of post-translational modification (e.g.
column2: phosphorylation) that occurs on or near the Nuclear Export Signal (NES)
column3: and its observed effect on NES activity or binding.

column1: #   Rationale: The passages state that CaMKI phosphorylation of CCTα residues impairs CRM1 binding
column2: directly linking a post-translational modification to the regulation of NES function and strength.

column1: # nes_canonical_sequence_match: Indicates whether the identified Nuclear Export Signal (NES) sequence matches a known canonical NES consensus sequence
column2: and notes any deviations or if it is considered atypical.

column3: The BRCA1 NES contains a series of large hydrophobic amino acids with a 3:2:1 spacing, which is analogous to that seen in other highly active Rev-type NESs.
column4: {'text': 'The BRCA1 NES contains a series of large hydrophobic amino acids with a 3:2:1 spacing (shaded areas) as previously observed in highly active NESs (22, 38).', 'source': 'BRCA1_10991937_full'} | {'text': 'Moreover, the 3:2:1 spacing of the critical hydrophobic residues is analogous to that seen in other highly active export sequences (22, 34, 38).', 'source': 'BRCA1_10991937_full'}
column7: The presence and function of the NES were determined using a combination of deletion analysis and a nuclear export assay involving a Rev(1.4)-GFP chimeric protein, as well as by observing the effect of leptomycin B (LMB) and CRM1 overexpression on BRCA1 localization.
column8: {'text': 'We identified three conserved motifs (81QLVEELLKIICAFQLDTGL) in BRCA1 that are similar to previously identified Rev-type NESs.', 'source': 'BRCA1_10991937_full'} | {'text': 'We next tested BRCA1 amino acid fragments 1–70 and 65–170 for export activity, using a new assay that enables identification of active NESs (22).', 'source': 'BRCA1_10991937_full'} | {'text': 'The BRCA1 sequence 81QLVEELLKIICAFQLDTGL was identified as a functional NES by using a combination of deletion analysis and a recently developed nuclear export assay (22).', 'source': 'BRCA1_10991937_full'} | {'text': 'Site-directed Mutagenesis of the NES Blocks Nuclear Export of Full-length BRCA1', 'source': 'BRCA1_10991937_full'} | {'text': 'The export inhibitor Leptomycin B Induces the Nuclear Accumulation of Ectopically Expressed BRCA1', 'source': 'BRCA1_10991937_full'} | {'text': 'Overexpression of the CRM1 Export Receptor Regulates Localization of Endogenous BRCA1', 'source': 'BRCA1_10991937_full'}
column10: {'text': 'We identified a functional Rev-type nuclear export sequence (81QLVEELLKIICAFQLDTGL) near the amino terminus of BRCA1 that facilitates export via the CRM1/exportin pathway.', 'source': 'BRCA1_10991937_full'}
column12: {'text': 'We identified a functional Rev-type nuclear export sequence (81QLVEELLKIICAFQLDTGL) near the amino terminus of BRCA1 that facilitates export via the CRM1/exportin pathway.', 'source': 'BRCA1_10991937_full'} | {'text': 'Moreover, we show that the NES mediates CRM1-dependent nuclear export of BRCA1, revealing a new regulatory pathway for BRCA1 localization.', 'source': 'BRCA1_10991937_full'}

column14: {'text': 'In the 1+ to 9+ scoring system for NES activity, the BRCA1 NES was rated as 5+.', 'source': 'BRCA1_10991937_full'} | {'text': 'Thus, the BRCA1 NES is weaker than the c-ABL NES (rated as 8+) but significantly stronger than the p53 and hdm2 export sequences (rated as 1+) (Fig. 5).', 'source': 'BRCA1_10991937_full'}
column15: Cell line-specific differences in the effect of LMB on BRCA1 localization suggest that BRCA1 export is a regulated process that may be influenced by the cell line.
column16: {'text': 'It is interesting to note that the LMB effect on GFP-Rev was similar in every cell line tested but that the degree of LMB-induced nuclear accumulation of BRCA1 varied among different cell lines, suggesting that the shuttling activity of BRCA1 may be regulated in a cell line-specific fashion.', 'source': 'BRCA1_10991937_full'} | {'text': 'This finding, combined with the cell line-specific differences in the effect of LMB on BRCA1 localization that we observed (Table II), suggests that BRCA1 export is a regulated process.', 'source': 'BRCA1_10991937_full'}
column17: The activity of the NES might be altered by post-translational modifications such as phosphorylation, or by binding of BRCA1 to other proteins.
column18: {'text': 'In this regard, the activity of the NES might be altered by post-translational modifications such as phosphorylation, which BRCA1 undergoes in a cell cycle-dependent fashion (14, 39), or after DNA damage (6), On the other hand, the proximity of the NES to the BRCA1 RING finger motif (9), a prominent site for protein-protein interactions, points to the possibility that the NES could become masked upon binding of BRCA1 to other proteins.', 'source': 'BRCA1_10991937_full'}
column19: The activity of the NES might be altered by post-translational modifications such as phosphorylation, which BRCA1 undergoes in a cell cycle-dependent fashion, or after DNA damage. column4: {'text': 'CaMKI docks within the CCTα membrane-binding domain (residues 290–299), a sequence that displays similarities to a canonical nuclear export signal (NES) that also binds CRM1/exportin 1.', 'source': 'CCTα_22621903_full'} | {'text': 'The D-site consensus motif consists of a basic cluster of one to four residues, a short spacer, and a hydrophobic-X-hydrophobic submotif ((R/K)3-5(X)1-5(Φ-X-Φ)).
